schema: The Actuals sheet (rows 3–55, header row 2, banner row 1) contains 53 ERP journal rows where Department codes follow the format DEPT-NNN and one row intentionally carries a trailing space, causing any formula that does not use TRIM() to silently miss that row. Revenue account codes begin with 4 and their Amount values in column E are stored as negative numbers, while all expense amounts are positive — this sign convention is the opposite of the Budget sheet, which stores every BudgetAmount (column D) as a positive number regardless of account type, so Revenue actuals and budget figures cannot be compared directly without taking ABS() of the actual. Account code 6100-01 does not exist as a parent row in Actuals; it appears only as sub-accounts 6100-01-A and 6100-01-B, whereas the Budget sheet records only the parent code 6100-01, meaning SUMIFS on Actuals against a parent code will correctly aggregate sub-account rows if a wildcard is used, but the Variance_Workings column D already holds parent codes that match Budget column B directly. The DeptMap sheet maps ERP DEPT-NNN codes (column A) to Budget D-prefix codes (column B), but one ERPCode row has a trailing space, so XLOOKUP or VLOOKUP lookups against DeptMap column A must wrap the lookup value in TRIM() to avoid a silent no-match. The Budget sheet (rows 3–44) contains no rows with a BudgetAmount of zero, which means a SUMIFS result of exactly 0 always indicates that no matching dept+account combination exists and must be treated as 'Not Budgeted' rather than a legitimate zero budget figure. Five dept+account combinations present in Variance_Workings rows 41–45 have no corresponding Budget row, and three combinations in rows 46–48 represent discontinued accounts that carry non-zero budget amounts but have zero actuals already pre-populated in column H. The Variance_Workings sheet has 47 data rows (rows 2–48); columns A, D, G, and H are pre-filled and read-only, and all learner-authored formulas reference those pre-filled values as inputs.

T01.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a positive integer count (never text, never blank) for every cell in H3:H55, representing how many times the JournalID in column A of that row appears across the entire locked range $A$3:$A$55. Cells where the JournalID is unique return 1; cells belonging to a duplicate pair return 2 — exactly two JournalIDs each appear twice in the dataset, so exactly four cells across H3:H55 will show 2 and all others will show 1. The range argument must be fully absolute ($A$3:$A$55) and the criteria argument must be the relative cell reference for the current row (e.g. A3) so the formula counts correctly when copied down. No IFERROR wrapper is needed or expected; the formula should never produce an error on clean numeric-string IDs.

T02.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a text Budget dept code in D-prefix format (e.g. 'D-SALES', 'D-MKTG') for every row in B2:B48 by looking up the ERPDeptCode from column A against DeptMap column A and returning the corresponding value from DeptMap column B. TRIM() must be applied to the lookup value (column A) to neutralise the one trailing-space ERPCode in DeptMap, otherwise that department's rows will silently return an error or fallback value instead of the correct D-prefix code. Because all 47 Variance_Workings rows have a valid ERP dept code that exists in DeptMap (after trimming), no row should return an error or fallback string — every cell in B2:B48 must contain a valid D-prefix text value. A formula that omits TRIM() will appear to work for 46 of 47 rows but will silently fail for the one row whose DeptMap entry has a trailing space.

T03.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a text account name (e.g. 'Salaries', 'Digital Advertising') for every cell in E2:E48 by looking up the AccountCode in column D against AccountMap column A and returning the corresponding AccountName from AccountMap column B. All account codes in column D are parent-level codes in NNNN-NN format and each has a matching row in AccountMap, so no cell should return an error or fallback under normal operation. If a learner uses a fallback argument of '#N/A' or similar, it should never appear in the expected output because every code resolves. The return type is always text, not a number, and no numeric transformation is required.

T05.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a positive number for rows 2–40 and rows 46–48 (where a matching Budget row exists) and must return the text string 'Not Budgeted' — not 0, not blank, not an error — for rows 41–45 where no Budget row matches. Because SUMIFS returns 0 when no rows match and the Budget sheet contains no legitimate zero amounts, a result of 0 from SUMIFS is the reliable signal for a missing row and must be intercepted with an IF(SUMIFS(...)=0, 'Not Budgeted', SUMIFS(...)) pattern; relying on IFERROR alone is insufficient because SUMIFS never errors on a no-match. The returned numbers are always positive (Budget stores all amounts as positive), and the five 'Not Budgeted' cells must contain the exact text string 'Not Budgeted' (case-sensitive as written) so that downstream IFERROR formulas in columns J and K can detect them as non-numeric. Any formula that returns 0 instead of 'Not Budgeted' for unmatched rows is incorrect and will cause silent errors in all dependent columns.

T06.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a number for every row: for rows where column I contains a valid budget number it returns the arithmetic difference H2 minus I2 (a positive result means actuals exceeded budget for expenses; negative means under-budget); for the five 'Not Budgeted' rows where column I contains text, subtracting text from a number would produce a #VALUE! error, so IFERROR must catch that error and return H2 (the raw actual amount) as the variance instead. The return type is always a number — never text, never blank, never an error visible to the user. Rows with discontinued accounts (rows 46–48) have zero actuals in H and positive budget in I, so their dollar variance will be a negative number. A formula that omits IFERROR will display #VALUE! in the five 'Not Budgeted' rows, which is incorrect.

T07.expectedBehavior: The formula must return a decimal number (e.g. 0.005, 0.2333) for rows where column I contains a numeric budget amount, representing the ratio J2 divided by I2, and must return the text string 'N/A' — not 0, not blank, not a #DIV/0! error — for any row where the division cannot be performed, specifically the five 'Not Budgeted' rows where I2 is text. IFERROR wrapping J2/I2 is the required pattern; because I2 is text in those rows the division produces a #VALUE! error which IFERROR catches and replaces with 'N/A'. The numeric results are raw decimal ratios and should be formatted as percentages in the spreadsheet, but the underlying cell value remains a decimal (e.g. 0.25 not 25). Rows 46–48 with zero actuals and non-zero budget will return a non-zero negative decimal, not 'N/A', because I2 is numeric in those rows.

T08.expectedBehavior: The formula must return the text 'Yes' or the text 'No' for every cell in L2:L48 with no blanks or errors. 'Yes' is returned only when all three conditions are simultaneously true: the absolute value of the dollar variance in J is greater than or equal to 25000, K is a number (not 'N/A'), and the absolute value of K is greater than or equal to 0.1 (10%). The ISNUMBER(K2) condition is mandatory — without it, rows where K2 is 'N/A' could incorrectly evaluate ABS('N/A') and produce an error or wrong result. Rows with 'Not Budgeted' in column I will always have 'N/A' in column K, so ISNUMBER gates them out and they always return 'No'. Every cell must return either 'Yes' or 'No' as text; no cell should be blank or show an error.

T09.expectedBehavior: The formula must return an empty string ('') for every row where L2 is not 'Yes', the text 'F' (Favorable) or 'U' (Unfavorable) only for rows marked 'Yes' in column L, and must never return a blank or error for any of the 47 rows. For Revenue rows (F2 = 'Revenue'), the comparison must use ABS(H2) against I2 because revenue actuals in column H are stored as negative numbers in the ERP; if ABS(H2) exceeds I2, actual revenue beat budget and the result is 'F', otherwise 'U'. For Expense rows, the direct comparison H2 < I2 is used (actual spend below budget = 'F', at or above budget = 'U'). Because only rows with L2 = 'Yes' receive an F/U label, the majority of rows will show an empty string, which is a valid and expected output — not a formula error.
